cards.title: TENS (Transcutaneous Electrical Nerve Stimulation)
cards._legacyHtml:
Mechanism: Gate control theory (high-freq) + endorphin release (low-freq).
Parameters:
  • Conventional (High-freq): 80–150 Hz, 10–50 µs pulse width, sensory intensity, 20–30 min — immediate onset analgesia.
  • Acupuncture-like (Low-freq): 1–4 Hz, 100–300 µs, motor intensity (visible twitch), 20–30 min — delayed but prolonged analgesia.
Electrode placement: Over or around painful area, along dermatome, or at motor points.
Indications: LBP, OA, post-operative pain, dysmenorrhoea, CRPS, neuropathic pain.
Contraindications: Pacemaker, epilepsy, over anterior neck, first trimester pregnancy, active bleeding.

cards.title: IFT (Interferential Therapy)
cards._legacyHtml:
Mechanism: Two medium-frequency AC currents (4000 Hz and 4001–4100 Hz) interfere within tissue, producing a low-frequency beat of 1–100 Hz for deep analgesic and anti-oedema effect.
Parameters: Carrier: 4000 Hz. Beat frequency: 80–100 Hz (analgesia); 1–10 Hz (muscle stimulation); 25–50 Hz (circulatory). Intensity: sensory–motor threshold. Duration: 15–20 min.
Electrode placement: 4-pole criss-cross over target area.
Advantage over TENS: Penetrates deeper with less skin impedance.
Indications: OA, sports injuries, post-traumatic swelling, LBP, shoulder impingement, urinary incontinence.

cards.title: Therapeutic Ultrasound
cards._legacyHtml:
Frequencies: 1 MHz (deep, 3–5 cm); 3 MHz (superficial, 1–2 cm).
Modes: Continuous (thermal) vs Pulsed (1:4 ratio — non-thermal, sub-acute/acute).
Intensity: 0.5–2.5 W/cm². Keep transducer moving at 3–4 cm/sec (prevents hot spots).
Duration: ERA (cm²) × 2 min per frequency level. Typically 5–10 min.
Effects: Thermal: ↑ tissue extensibility, ↑ blood flow, pain relief. Non-thermal: cavitation + acoustic streaming → ↑ tissue repair, collagen synthesis.
Indications: Tendinopathies, ligament sprains, scar tissue, bursitis, plantar fasciitis.
CI: Over epiphysis in children, malignancy, metallic implants, pacemaker.

cards.title: Shortwave Diathermy (SWD)
cards._legacyHtml:
Frequency: 27.12 MHz (ISM band). Penetration: 3–5 cm.
Types:
  • Condenser (Capacitive): Pads placed on each side of limb — heats soft tissue
  • Inductance (Inductive): Drum/cable electrode — heats muscle preferentially
Modes: Continuous (thermal) vs Pulsed (PSWD — non-thermal, anti-inflammatory).
Duration: 15–20 min. Intensity: mild to moderate warmth.
Indications: Subacute/chronic joint conditions, muscle spasm, sinusitis (PSWD), pelvic inflammatory conditions.
CI: Metallic implants, pacemaker, wet dressings, ischaemic tissue, malignancy, pregnancy.

cards.title: LASER Therapy (Low-Level Laser Therapy — LLLT)
cards._legacyHtml:
Wavelengths: 630–700 nm (red, superficial) or 780–950 nm (near-infrared, deep 3–5 cm).
Power: 10–500 mW. Dose: 1–4 J/cm² for acute; 4–8 J/cm² for chronic.
Mechanism: Photobiomodulation — cytochrome c oxidase absorbs photons → ↑ ATP, ↓ reactive oxygen species, ↑ collagen synthesis, ↑ NGF.
Application: Hold probe perpendicular, contact or 0.5 cm, scan at 1 cm/sec or point probe.
Duration: 2–10 min per site. Course: 8–12 sessions.
Indications: Wound healing, tendinopathies, OA, neuropathic pain, oral ulcers, lymphoedema.
CI: Over malignancy, eye exposure, thyroid, pregnancy.

cards.title: Traction Therapy
cards._legacyHtml:
Types: Manual, Mechanical (motorised), Positional, Auto-traction.
Cervical Traction: Force: 7–15 kg (10% body weight). Angle: 20–30° flexion. Duration: 10–20 min. Mode: intermittent preferred (3:1 on:off). Indications: cervical disc prolapse, nerve root compression, cervicogenic headache.
Lumbar Traction: Force: 25–50% body weight. Duration: 15–25 min. Patient supine, hips/knees flexed. Indications: LBP, lumbar disc prolapse (L4–S1), sciatica.
Contraindications: RA (cervical instability), spinal cord compression, osteoporosis, hypermobility, acute disc prolapse with neurological deficit, aortic aneurysm.

cards.title: Maitland Mobilisation — Grades
cards._legacyHtml:
Grade I: Small amplitude at beginning of range. Use: severe pain / acute.
Grade II: Large amplitude within range, not reaching limit. Use: pain + stiffness.
Grade III: Large amplitude reaching end of range with resistance. Use: stiffness with mild pain.
Grade IV: Small amplitude at end of range with resistance. Use: stiffness predominant.
Grade V: Thrust (HVLA) — high velocity, small amplitude, at end of range (manipulation).
Rule: Grades I–II: pain dominant. Grades III–IV: stiffness dominant. Always reassess after each grade. Grades I–IV = mobilisation; Grade V = manipulation.

cards.title: MET (Muscle Energy Technique)
cards._legacyHtml:
Definition: Osteopathic technique where patient uses their muscles on request against a precisely controlled counterforce from the therapist to lengthen shortened muscles or mobilise restricted joints.
PIR (Post-Isometric Relaxation): Take muscle to barrier → patient contracts 20–25% for 7–10 sec → relax → therapist stretches to new barrier. Repeat 3–4 times. Use: muscle shortening, trigger points.
RI (Reciprocal Inhibition): Contract antagonist against resistance → agonist relaxes. Use: pain inhibition, acute spasm.
Indications: Hamstring tightness, cervical restriction, sacroiliac dysfunction, shoulder hypomobility, piriformis syndrome.

cards.title: Myofascial Release (MFR)
cards._legacyHtml:
Principle: Sustained, low-load stretching of fascial restrictions to allow viscoelastic and piezoelectric change in the ground substance of connective tissue.
Direct MFR: Find fascial restriction → apply sustained pressure directly into the barrier → hold 90–120 sec → feel the release (tissue softening or piezoconductance).
Indirect MFR: Move away from barrier into ease → hold → await inherent release. Gentler; used for acute or fragile patients.
Fascial chains target: Posterior superficial line (hamstrings→erectors), lateral line (ITB), spiral line (obliques).
Indications: Chronic pain, postural dysfunction, fibromyalgia, post-surgical adhesions, headache.

cards.title: Trigger Point Therapy
cards._legacyHtml:
Definition: Hyperirritable spot in a taut band of skeletal muscle — tender on pressure, refers pain in a predictable pattern (Travell & Simons map).
Techniques:
  • Ischaemic compression: Sustained thumb pressure 30–90 sec at trigger point → blanching → release.
  • Spray & Stretch: Vapocoolant spray → passive stretch — for acute/sensitive TrPs.
  • Dry needling: Fine needle into TrP until local twitch response.
Key TrPs: Upper trapezius → temporal headache. Piriformis → sciatic-like pain. Quadratus lumborum → LBP. Infraspinatus → anterior shoulder/arm pain.

cards.title: Hot Packs (Moist Heat Therapy)
cards._legacyHtml:
Temperature: 40–45°C at skin surface. Heating duration: 15–20 min.
Types: Hydrocollator packs (silica gel), electric heating pad, moist towel.
Effects: ↑ local blood flow (reactive hyperaemia), ↓ muscle spasm, ↑ tissue extensibility, ↓ pain (counter-irritant + gate control), ↓ joint stiffness.
Application: Always use 6–8 layers of towelling between pack and skin. Check at 5 min for burns.
Indications: Chronic muscle spasm, subacute/chronic arthritis, contractures, joint stiffness (pre-exercise).
CI: Acute inflammation, peripheral vascular disease, impaired sensation, open wounds, malignancy, first 48h post-injury.

cards.title: Cold Pack / Cryotherapy
cards._legacyHtml:
Temperature: 10–15°C at skin surface. Duration: 10–20 min (RICE protocol 20 min on/20 off).
Types: Ice pack, ice massage, cold towel, cryo-cuff, vapocoolant spray.
Physiological Stages (CBAN): Cold → Burning → Aching → Numbness. Treatment endpoint at Numbness stage.
Effects: Vasoconstriction → ↓ oedema, ↓ bleeding, ↓ nerve conduction → analgesia, ↓ spasticity (brief), ↓ metabolic demand.
Indications: Acute injuries (first 48–72 h), muscle spasm (spastic type), acute gout, post-surgical swelling, headache.
CI: Raynaud's disease, cold urticaria, peripheral vascular disease, impaired sensation.

cards.title: Paraffin Wax Bath
cards._legacyHtml:
Temperature: 47–54°C (safe because low thermal conductivity).
Methods:
  • Dip & wrap: Dip hand 7–12 times → wrap in plastic then towel → retain 15–20 min → peel off. Best for hands.
  • Immersion: Submerge limb in wax bath for 20–30 min.
  • Paint method: Brush on for irregular surfaces (feet, face).
Effects: Concentrated heat to superficial structures; ↑ skin extensibility; ↑ local circulation; ↓ stiffness; analgesic.
Indications: Rheumatoid arthritis (subacute/chronic), OA hands, scleroderma, Raynaud's (chronic), post-fracture stiffness.
CI: Open wounds, infections, acute inflammation, heat hypersensitivity.

cards.title: Contrast Bath
cards._legacyHtml:
Protocol: Two basins — hot (40–43°C) and cold (10–18°C). Begin and end with hot.
Standard Ratio: 3–4 min hot : 1 min cold. Repeat 4–5 cycles. Total: 20–25 min.
Mechanism: Alternating vasodilation and vasoconstriction creates 'vascular pump' → ↑ lymphatic drainage, ↓ oedema, ↑ nutrient delivery, ↓ waste products.
Indications: Sub-acute/chronic oedema, ankle sprains (after 72 h), CRPS (early), peripheral circulatory insufficiency, post-immobilisation stiffness.
CI: Acute injuries (<48 h), DVT, peripheral vascular disease, impaired temperature sensation, open wounds.
